title: Old Carousel Gets a New Spin — and a Civil Rights Story
summary:
Have you ever ridden a carousel that has its own history lesson? The Smithsonian National Carousel on the National Mall in Washington, D.C., just reopened after three years of restoration. But this carousel isn't just about pretty horses — it's a symbol of the fight for equality.

Back in the 1960s, this carousel was at Gwynn Oak Amusement Park near Baltimore. At that time, Black people were not allowed to ride. But brave kids and adults — both Black and white — protested for years. Finally, on August 28, 1963, the park opened to everyone. That was the very same day Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. gave his famous "I Have a Dream" speech!

Sharon Langley was the first Black child to ride that day. She was only 11 months old! Now 63, she rode again this week on a horse named "Freedom Rider." She said the carousel belongs with all the monuments of freedom.

Today, the carousel has 54 horses, a sea monster, a pig, and two chariots. Kids love it. Seven-year-old Lucas Platt said, "It's actually one of the fastest carousels I've really been on."

So next time you see a merry-go-round, remember: sometimes even a fun ride can be part of a big, important story.
why_it_matters: This story reminds us that even fun things like carousels can be part of the fight for fairness, and that everyone should be allowed to enjoy the same things.

keywords.explanation: Fixing up something old to make it new again.
Something that stands for a bigger idea.
Carriages pulled by horses on a carousel.
Treating everyone the same no matter their skin color.
Showed strong disagreement by gathering or marching.
The park where the carousel used to be before it moved to Washington, D.C.

questions.question: Why is this carousel special?
options: It was part of the civil rights movement
It's the fastest carousel in the world
questions.correct_answer: It was part of the civil rights movement

questions.question: Who was the first Black child to ride the carousel?
What famous speech was given on the same day the park opened to everyone?
options: 'Ask Not What Your Country Can Do'

questions.question: What was the name of the horse Sharon Langley rode this time?
How old was Sharon Langley when she first rode the carousel in 1963?
Where is the Smithsonian National Carousel located?
options: On the National Mall in Washington, D.C.
In a theme park in Florida
questions.correct_answer: On the National Mall in Washington, D.C.

background_read: In the 1960s, many places in the United States were segregated, meaning Black and white people had to use separate facilities.
The civil rights movement worked to change that and make things fair for everyone.
This carousel was at an amusement park that did not allow Black children to ride until protesters forced it to open.
Article_Structure: WHO: Sharon Langley, the first Black child to ride the carousel, and Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. (mentioned for his speech).
WHAT: The carousel reopened after three years of restoration; it has a civil rights history.
WHERE: The Smithsonian National Carousel on the National Mall in Washington, D.C.
WHY: To preserve the history of the fight for equality and let families enjoy the carousel together.

perspectives.description: The carousel is a reminder of the struggle for civil rights and how far the country has come.
Now families of all backgrounds can enjoy the carousel together, which is a celebration of inclusion.
The carousel helps teach kids about history in a fun way, making the past come alive.
